author: @alericmarx1233
text:
I was watching your sleazy tricks video earlier, I too believe that people are inherently good.

I like how your using the flatland theory. Math is a great tool to measure our universe.

As far as my understanding of the universe, our reality is no more than the accumulation of our interpretations and understanding using our inherent tools.
We constantly develop and innovate upon our tools to reach for better comprehension and use. I feel we have many small, but substantial epiphany's if you will as we gather knowledge that can transform our understanding.

I feel our expansion of Global communication is shinning a light on many of the darker or misunderstood corners of the world and raising a great awareness. 

I really enjoy your videos Robert!

author: @timbrereton1896
text:
Robert it takes courage to speak up and begin a conversation on the subjects you raise because it is so easy to criticize from an armchair, a lofty intellectual position, and a computer keyboard rather than do what you do which is to actually make an effort and videotape your research and your musings. You have incredible levels of both practical science, chemistry, and theory and I think your videos are great because you prompt people to be curious, and search for answers.And you do it without arrogance. You do not make the mistake the US patent office made over 100 years ago were their leadership wanted to shut the US patent office down because every thing that could be invented was already invented and there was no more need for a patent office. Hilarious but it shows the foolishness of believing that everything to be known is already known. Various sections of science and education appear to have a pride problem in my opinion and view themselves as the guardians of all truth battling the barbarians ( non academic researchers and inventors) at the gates of acceptance. They even battle each other to be the arbiters and regulators of all reality. As you point out mathematics is a language that attempts to model reality, but it is based on variables that can change for various reasons.

In some ways the greatest scientific and inventing minds in history remind me of the 6 blind men of India who rubbed up against different parts of an elephant and claimed they had something totally else. The man who grabbed the tail thought He had a snake, the others claimed other components but none of them realized they were just experiencing different components of the same elephant. Nicola Tesla and others of his age believed in the Ether, Einstein believed in a Unified Field but could not finish a mathematical model because He could not figure out how gravity fit in. Is it possible the ether and the unified field are the same thing, just not perfectly understood? Maybe, maybe not.

But Tesla viewed Gravity as an effect of electromagnetism and was not a stand alone force in the universe. Academia also conveniently forgets that although Einstein had undertaken a four-year mathematics and physics teaching diploma program at the Zürich Polytechnic, Einstein was working as a patent clerk/examiner in Switzerland when He wrote his formula for E=mc2. Academia is an important center of knowledge, but so are open innovation networks, as many of Einstein's ideas were formulated amongst  a open innovation forum called the Olympia Academy which Einstein started.

In reality many of the scientific greats were experimenters first (sometimes using their mind only) who acted out on observing natural phenemona then postulating theories and conducting experiments. In many, many instances it was only after successfull experiments that mathematical models were formulated later that explained the reason why reality was functioning in the way it was with the variables that were in place both in the natural world and in the "successfull" experiments.

Micheal Faraday who was one of the greatest scientific minds in history, postulated then conducted physical experiments to prove out whether his postulations and theories were accurate or mistaken. He would test multiple variables thus creating a  new standard in testing procedures still used today. It was only later that Maxwell wrote mathematical formulas to explain Faraday's successes and the natural processes and systems he investigated and researched. Faraday had a practical basic form of algebra, but His mathematical ideas followed on from his practical experiments and data collection.

Mathematics are obviously important, and in cases were experiments are difficult to conduct mathematics are crucial especially in physics. At the same time in many cases in actual scientific breakthrough history, the math is formulated after the discovery to explain what is already occurring in reality. As one Professor  said to me jokingly years ago" Yes it works in reality, but it will NEVER work in Theory". He was trying to jokingly point out that some mathematicians believe the universe cannot work without their approval and formulas rather than their actual role which is to formulate math models that try to explain what is already happening. Every component of science has its limitations and it is the explorers and experimenters who sail into unkown territory only to be told by "experts" that they are wasting their time. And many times the "experts" are correct. But when they are wrong they are really wrong as someone makes a breakthrough discovery which changes the accepted paradigm. Way to go Robert, keep it up, as I as well as many others find value in your videos and the comments posted from your viewers.

author: @dedlunch
text: Totally, absolutely agree, math is just a language, a conceptual thing, and it seems like a lot of people don't apply it with reason, and given the way you write an equation, certain ways will prove one thing and another way, proving something paradoxical. I remember in school, I got straightAway's in algebra, but told I was cheating, because I couldn't show my work the way they did it. The way I solved the problems, me showing them my way of doing it made no sense to them. But the answers were correct most of the time.

author: @erlendse
text:
Is it intentional to say universe instead of for example context?

Most physics stuff use conditions that do not really exsist like a flat friction free surface, a straight line etc. They rarely actually are if you do bigger or smaller scale.

The error is usually too small to matter, but not always.

Also taking stuff from one context to another should be done with care, like working with soft vs hard material.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: erlendse yes an example context is a subset of a universe whereas a universe is a non extensible environment where the restricted rules hold true for all extensions of the imagined universe they operate in - or at least that's the way i see it

author: @NeoShameMan
text: I have been thinking along the same line, For example I tried to find a system where 1+1 = 3 (obviously procreation could be one) that still make sense mathematically, however the extra 1 created a loop that make any addition infinite ... until I found out that's already done, we use function f(1) + f(2) = 3 is another way to note non linear math , by defining the function to be projected onto linear math, just like we represent non euclidean space by projecting them in euclidean space. Thinking outside the box also give a better appreciation of the box! And the correct notation highlight the correct (self contain ie it's own universe) logic!

replies.author: @dekutree64
replies.text: This is a common misunderstanding of Tesla. His system used ground currents. Not faster than light, just traveling in a straight line through the earth instead of going around the surface. Diameter is 2*r, whereas half the circumference is pi*r, so the wavefront arrives at the opposite side in 2/pi the time it would take to go around.

replies.author: @dekutree64
replies.text: Also, in Tesla coils, part of the energy propagates straight up the coil, jumping from one turn to the next instead of going round and round. It looks faster than light if you measure relative to the length of the wire, but it's still <c in actual 3D space. Pythagoras wins again :)

author: @FredGandt
text:
I'm right _there_ with ya Rob!

I've been bashing my head against the brick wall of this issue for most of my life (by proportion). But the average joe doesn't react well to the suggestion that not being able to measure something, doesn't mean it isn't there.
I am astounded by how many self proclaimed _scientists_ spend an inordinate amount of time _beleiving_ that the _knowledge_ of the here and now, is uberversally true, and arguing vehemently against anything that could be construed _"questionable"_.
Science is entirely about questions and the best science makes no claims of having any answers. If it sees something, it makes notes about how, where, when _etc._ and posits some possible explanations (which provide the next round of questions), but would never sink so low as to claim to have sussed it.
I'm saddened by the attitude summed up by comments I saw on an earlier video of yours in this recent spurt, that in essence, philosophising was rubbish and only maths could be trusted, and then chuckled when I saw the title of this video :-)
I doubt Galileo designed and built a telescope and then noticed the sky.
The idea that _"thinking outside the box"_ isn't _"proper science"_ is just plain bloody ridiculous!

It's 4am and I'm not sure what I'm typing is relevant or even English, so I'll be quiet now.

I for one enjoy your not so conformist delvings, and like all good scientists, look forward to the results, whatever they may be.

https://plus.google.com/u/0/+FredGandt/posts/CdX8Mjh4Jtg <-- Look at the date ;-)


EDIT (for P.S.):


Also https://plus.google.com/u/0/+FredGandt/posts/B6BANzst4Xd and https://plus.google.com/u/0/+FredGandt/posts/dj9iBqxuApo and https://plus.google.com/u/0/+FredGandt/posts/DmQF22PouuU for pudding.


Ni-night >.-
